data.title: Mace calls for Bondi to testify in Epstein files probe or be held in contempt
data.description:
Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.) on Wednesday echoed Democratic calls to hold former Attorney General Pam Bondi in contempt if she failed to comply with a subpoena to testify before the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee about the Jeffrey Epstein files.  

The Justice Department told House Oversight Committee Chair James Comer (R-Ky.) Wednesday that Bondi would not appear for the scheduled April 14 deposition. 

Mace, who introduced the measure to subpoena Bondi, got the backing of four GOP colleagues and all the Democrats on the panel in a vote last month. 

“We expect Pam Bondi’s deposition to be rescheduled in a timely fashion. Our motion made clear the Committee must issue a subpoena to Pam Bondi, not the occupant of the office of Attorney General of the United States,” Mace wrote on social platform X. 

“Coordinate with her personal attorney, issue an updated subpoena if necessary. But if Pam Bondi continues to refuse to comply, she should be held in contempt.”

Rep. Robert Garcia (Calif.), the top Democrat on the committee, said Wednesday that the party would pursue contempt if Bondi did not comply. 

“Our bipartisan subpoena is to Pam Bondi, whether she is the Attorney General or not. She must come in to testify immediately, and if she defies the subpoena, we will begin contempt charges in the Congress,” he said in a statement. 

The Department of Justice argued in a letter to Comer obtained by The Hill that Bondi should not have to appear now that she has been fired. 

“Ms. Bondi no longer holds that office. As a result, because Ms. Bondi no longer can testify in her official capacity as Attorney General, the Department’s position is that the subpoena no longer obligates her to appear,” Assistant Attorney General Patrick Davis wrote.

The panel’s Democrats have refuted that argument, noting that Bondi was subpoenaed by name to sit for a deposition with the panel, as were other former attorneys general.

Former Attorney General Bill Barr, for instance, sat for a deposition last year. 

It is unlawful to defy a subpoena, and the panel could seek to escalate the matter by voting to refer the matter to the Justice Department for criminal prosecution, something the full House would have to back through a contempt motion. 

Even if such a measure did advance, it would rest with President Trump’s Justice Department whether to file charges.

data.title: Questions surround Strait of Hormuz opening as Iran contradicts US
data.description:
Conflicting messages from U.S. and Iranian officials about the status of the Strait of Hormuz are raising questions about what’s actually happening with the key oil shipping lane.

The waterway has largely been closed since American and Israeli attacks on Iran began nearly six weeks ago.

President Trump has said that access to the strait, through which about 20 percent of the world’s oil typically flows on an average day, is a cornerstone of the tenuous two-week ceasefire with Iran.

The mixed signals from Washington and Tehran may worsen already rising tensions. 

“Both sides are still at odds over the contours of what…a negotiated settlement is going to be,” said Andrew Lipow, president of  consulting firm Lipow Oil Associates.

American officials on Tuesday night and Wednesday vowed that the strait will be opened without restrictions.

Trump on Tuesday said that he would “suspend” attacks on Iran for two weeks if the country agreed to the “COMPLETE, IMMEDIATE, and SAFE OPENING of the Strait of Hormuz.” Iran’s leadership on Tuesday said it agreed to terms.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt reiterated Wednesday that the “ceasefire is subject to the free, safe and immediate reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.”

She added that this means “without limitation, including tolls.”

And, she said that the U.S. would “hold” Iran to reopening the strait without restrictions. 

Ship tracking service Marine Traffic reported early Wednesday that there were some signs of movement and that two vessels had successfully traversed the strait.

However, Tehran has telegraphed that it would continue charging tolls and would seek to remain in control of the waterway off its shores.

A senior Iranian official told Reuters that Iran could open the strait in a way that is “limited, under ‌Iran’s ⁠control,” on Thursday or Friday before a meeting with U.S. officials on Saturday in Pakistan.

A spokesperson for Iran’s Oil, Gas and Petrochemical Products Exporters’ Union, which works with Iran’s government, told the Financial Times, meanwhile, that Iran would impose a toll of $1 per barrel of oil — in cryptocurrency — on ships seeking to move through the Strait.

And the Wall Street Journal reported that Iran told mediators that during the ceasefire it would restrict passage to around 12 ships per day and would collect fees for that privilege.

But Iran may have gone even further on Wednesday, closing the strait entirely after Israel attacked Lebanon, according to Iranian state media. 

Israel’s military said Wednesday that it struck more than 100 Hezbollah-linked military sites. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s office said that Lebanon was not part of the ceasefire, an understanding that Vice President Vance later backed up.

At a midday White House press briefing, Leavitt, however, decried the report that the strait was closed as “false.” 

“The president was made aware of those reports before I came to the podium, that is completely unacceptable and again, this is a case of what they’re saying publicly is different [than] privately,” Leavitt told reporters. “We have seen an uptick of traffic in the strait today.” 

She did not answer directly a question about who was in control of the Strait of Hormuz.

All in all, the comments paint a mixed picture about the status of the oil shipping channel off Iran’s coast.

Oil prices were significantly down on Wednesday following the announcement of the ceasefire, which came less than two hours before Trump’s deadline for Iran to open the strait or face punishing attacks.

U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate was down about 15 percent, while international benchmark Brent crude was down about 12 percent. 

Lipow added that what ultimately happens with prices will depend on whether both sides can bridge their apparent gaps. 

“The oil market is down today on the expectation that the ceasefire will be the start of a process that allows more oil to reach the market,” he said, adding that he’s concerned that “this might just be a pause, rather than something that leads to… a resolution of the conflict.”

As oil prices sink, gasoline prices could soon follow, though the national average price was still high on Wednesday at about $4.16 per gallon. 

Prices at the pump have soared by more than $1 per gallon since the conflict began, creating pain for consumers and a political headache for the Trump administration.

data.title: 4 big questions hanging over Trump's ceasefire with Iran
data.description:
Within 24 hours this week, President Trump jumped from threats to wipe out Iran’s civilization to announcing that Tehran had presented a plan that led him to agree to a 14-day ceasefire — less than two hours before his deadline to attack Iranian infrastructure.

About 24 hours later, the ceasefire is already showing severe cracks, with Iran continuing to fire missiles and drones at Gulf states on Wednesday and Israel pounding Lebanon with waves of airstrikes. Iran has threatened to keep the Strait of Hormuz closed until Israel halts its strikes in Lebanon, jeopardizing a key aspect of the ceasefire.

The status of the strait is one of many unanswered questions around the agreement. U.S. and Iranian officials have also sent mixed messages on the future of Iran’s nuclear material and what “reopening” the Strait of Hormuz will actually mean. 

Here’s four big questions hanging over the ceasefire. 

Will Israel’s attacks on Lebanon blow up the deal?

In its 10-point proposal to end the war, Iran demanded that fighting in Lebanon end, but both Trump and Vice President Vance, who played a key role in negotiations, say the U.S.-Iranian ceasefire does not include its operation against Hezbollah.

That contradicts a statement from Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, who said on Tuesday night the ceasefire does include Lebanon.

Either way, Israel’s continued campaign against Lebanon threatens to unravel the delicate deal. 

In Lebanon on Wednesday, Israel launched its most significant military assault since it began fighting Tehran-backed Hezbollah militants, with dozens of strikes reported in the south and east of the country, including in the densely populated capital of Beirut, killing a reported 254 people. 

“The Iran-U.S. Ceasefire terms are clear and explicit: the U.S. must choose—ceasefire or continued war via Israel. It cannot have both,” Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said in a post on X. “The world sees the massacres in Lebanon. The ball is in the U.S. court, and the world is watching whether it will act on its commitments.”

Haneen Sayed, Lebanon’s minister of social affairs, condemned Israel’s strike, telling The Associated Press that the attacks are a “very dangerous turning point.”

Will Iran reopen the Strait of Hormuz?

Trump has maintained that the ceasefire hinges on the Strait of Hormuz, through which one-fifth of the world’s crude oil passes through.  

Iran had closed the shipping lane shortly after U.S.-Israeli attacks on the country on Feb. 28, allowing only a few ships to pass through for a fee and creating a global energy crisis that sent oil and gas prices soaring.

In a statement on the ceasefire deal, Iranian officials said for the two-week period, “safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz will be possible via coordination with Iran’s Armed Forces and with due consideration of technical limitations.”  

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Wednesday at the Pentagon that the strait was open, but only hours later, Iran again closed the strait in response to Israeli attacks in Lebanon. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told journalists reports of the strait being closed were “false,” adding, “this is a case of what they’re saying publicly is different, privately.”

According to maritime monitors, only three ships passed through the strait on Wednesday. Should it open back up, it’s unclear if Iran will seek to charge ships passing through. 

The regime has floated the idea that they would enact a “toll booth” system in the waterway, exerting a price on ships seeking safe passage through the strait to the tune of $1 per barrel of oil passing through, to be paid in cryptocurrency, the Financial Times reported.

Iranian officials told The Wall Street Journal it would allow 12 ships a day to pass through during the ceasefire, compared to more than 100 per day before the war. 

Separately, Trump told ABC News he is “thinking of” controlling the strait via a “joint venture” with Tehran to enact tolls in the waterway.

Will Iran’s military comply with ceasefire?

Despite the ceasefire, Gulf states including Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates reported new Iranian attacks on Wednesday, indicating that missiles and drones were still being launched from Iran. 

The Qatari defense ministry said its armed forces intercepted seven ballistic missiles and several drones from Iranian territory, while Kuwait said its ‌air defenses ​stopped ​a wave of ⁠Iranian ​drones — with some targeting major infrastructure including oil ​facilities, ​power ⁠stations and ​water desalination ​plants.

Saudi Arabia said it intercepted nine drones. 

Leavitt boasted during the White House briefing that the U.S. had destroyed the Iranian military’s command and control capacity. Hegseth, during a Pentagon briefing later in the day, suggested that could be to blame for the continued Iranian attacks. 

“Iran would be wise to find a way to get the carrier pigeons to their troops out in remote locations to know not to shoot, not to shoot any longer, one-way [drone] attacks of missiles, because it takes time sometimes for a ceasefire to take hold,” he said. “We’re watching it. We’re prepared if necessary. We hope, we believe that it’ll hold.”

Iran also appears ready to again quickly take up arms should agreements fall through, with Major General Mohsen Rezaei, a senior official and former IRGC commander, declaring Wednesday that Iran’s armed forces remain at the ready until the regime’s interests are secured.

“The hand of the armed forces remains on the trigger until the complete protection of the state’s interests is ensured,” he said in a post on X, according to the semi-official Fars News Agency.

Will Iran give up nuclear material buried underground?

Iran does not appear to have given any commitments to hand over its uranium enrichment or nuclear material, believed to be buried in the rubble of a facility bombed by the US this summer.

However, both Hegseth and Trump expressed confidence Wednesday that Iran would give up the weapons-grade material, a key aim of many Iran hawks. 

Trump in a Truth Social post Wednesday morning declared, “There will be no enrichment of Uranium, and the United States will, working with Iran, dig up and remove all of the deeply buried (B-2 Bombers) Nuclear ‘Dust.’”

Trump has vowed to stop Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon, insisting the war was necessary to do so.  

His Pentagon chief later on Tuesday suggested that Tehran had agreed to remove enriched uranium as part of the temporary truce.

“They know this agreement means that they will never, ever assess a nuclear weapon,” Hegseth said. “Under the terms, any nuclear material they should have will be removed. Their dust is deeply buried and watched 24/7 overhead.”

The United Nation’s nuclear watchdog, the International Atomic Energy Agency, in February estimated that as of June 2025, Iran had amassed about 970 pounds of highly enriched uranium — material that could be used to make several nuclear bombs.

Pakistani mediators have pressed both sides to start talks in Islamabad this weekend to sort out their remaining differences on a long-term peace deal.

data.title: Swalwell campaign denies 'outrageous' claims of inappropriate behavior
data.description:
Rep. Eric Swalwell’s (D-Calif.) campaign for governor of California has denied claims circulating on social media that multiple women plan to come forward with allegations of sexual misconduct against the lawmaker, just weeks ahead of the Golden State’s closely watched primary. 

“This false, outrageous rumor is being spread 27 days before an election begins by flailing opponents who have sadly teamed up with MAGA conspiracy theorists because they know Eric Swalwell is the frontrunner in this race,” a Swalwell campaign spokesperson told The Hill in a statement. 

The campaign also denied claims that some women have refrained from coming forward because of non-disclosure agreements (NDA). 

“In 13 years, no one in Eric Swalwell’s Congressional office has ever been asked to sign an NDA. Ever. In 13 years, not a single ethics complaint by any staff in his office or any other office has ever been lodged. Ever,” the spokesperson said, adding that Swalwell has “spent his career fighting for women” in the House and would continue to do so as governor. 

Cheyenne Hunt, an attorney and former California congressional candidate who is now the executive director of Gen-Z for Change, said in a Monday post on the social media platform X that she has been “working with a number of women who are in the process of coming forward and sharing their stories of sexual harassment and even alleged abuse at the hands of Eric Swalwell.” 

Arielle Fodor, an education influencer who goes by “Mrs. Frazzled” online, also said that women have come forward with claims.

Though the identities of the women and their claims have not been verified by The Hill or any major outlet, Fodor and Hunt have signaled they plan to go public with the information soon. They’ve also forcefully denied the suggestion that they are operatives making a political play. 

Hunt, in an apparent response to the Swalwell campaign’s statement as shared by other outlets, said in a post on X that “smearing survivors with claims that they ‘teamed up with MAGA’ is morally repugnant.” 

The developments come as Swalwell runs in the crowded race to replace term-limited Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) in California. Polls showed him climbing to the front of the non-partisan primary field in recent weeks, and a recent clash with the Trump administration was seen as a potential political boost as the June contest nears.

It was reported last week that the FBI has considered releasing files on a suspected Chinese spy who interacted with Swalwell, who was not accused of wrongdoing. The lawmaker argued the move would not only abuse his privacy but violate Justice Department guidelines around taking actions close to an election.

Swalwell is running for governor against Republican former Fox News host Steve Hilton, Republican Riverside County Sheriff Chad Bianco, Democrat billionaire Tom Steyer, former Rep. Katie Porter (D) and others. All candidates will appear on the ballot in a nonpartisan June 2 primary, and the top two vote-getters will face off in the November general election.

As a competitive Democratic field splinters base support, experts have raised the possibility that two Republicans advance in the deep blue stronghold, which last voted for a Republican governor in 2006, when former Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger (R) won reelection.

Mail ballots go out for the primary early next month.

data.title: RFK Jr. launching podcast in 'transparency' push
data.description:
Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is launching a podcast that, in his words, will focus on “telling the truth, especially when it’s uncomfortable” and “confronting the spiritual malaise.”

Kennedy is no stranger to podcasts, often appearing on shows like “The Joe Rogan Experience” and “Club Random with Bill Maher.” In a similar vein, Kennedy’s podcast will be titled “The Secretary Kennedy Podcast.”

“For decades now, Americans have been told that we should trust the system, but our children are sicker. Chronic Disease is exploding, and the answers that we’ve been given aren’t working. Many of us have come to the conclusion that government actually lies to us,” Kennedy said in a video posted on the social media platform X.

“This podcast is about telling the truth, especially when it’s uncomfortable. I’m going to have fearless conversations with critical thinkers, including independent doctors, respected scientists and leaders in medical innovation and research,” he added. “I’m going to ask the questions and lift the taboos and expose the hypocrisy and the conflicts and the corruption.”

This new project comes amid several setbacks for Kennedy’s “Make America Healthy Again” agenda, including a federal judge blocking his childhood immunization schedule and finding that most of the members he appointed to an influential vaccine advisory committee were unqualified.

There have also been mounting reports of the White House becoming disinterested in Kennedy’s push to overhaul vaccine policy, with sources telling Stat last month that the administration was “done” with vaccines.

“The Secretary Kennedy Podcast will provide a direct platform to engage with the public on the health issues that matter most to American families, from chronic disease and nutrition to food quality and health care costs, while bringing radical transparency to the conversations shaping those issues,” HHS spokesman Andrew Nixon said in a statement to The Hill.

data.title: Vance blames 'legitimate misunderstanding' for dispute over whether Lebanon included in ceasefire
data.description:
Vice President Vance on Wednesday said there was a “legitimate misunderstanding” on the ceasefire agreement with Iran after its leaders slammed the U.S. for an Israeli attack on Lebanon. 

“I think this comes from a legitimate misunderstanding,” Vance told reporters on Wednesday. 

“I think that the Iranians thought that the ceasefire included Lebanon, and it just didn’t. We never made that promise,” he added.

” … that said, the Israelis as I understand it … have actually offered to frankly check themselves a little bit in Lebanon because they want to make sure that our negotiation is successful.”

Pakistan’s Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif and Iran’s Speaker of Parliament Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf both said the ceasefire was supposed to include Lebanon, where the Iran-backed group Hezbollah is based. However, President Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu both said Wednesday that Lebanon was not a part of the agreement. 

“With the greatest humility, I am pleased to announce that the Islamic Republic of Iran and the United States of America, along with their allies, have agreed to an immediate ceasefire everywhere including Lebanon and elsewhere, EFFECTIVE IMMEDIATELY,” Sharif wrote in a Tuesday post on X, announcing the deal’s framework. 

In an interview Wednesday, however, Trump said Lebanon was not a part of the ceasefire.

“They were not included in the deal. That’ll get taken care of too. It’s all right,” the president said. 

When asked by PBS if he was OK with Israel continuing to hit Lebanon, Trump said, “it’s part of the deal.”

“Everyone knows that,” he said. “That’s a separate skirmish.” 

Iran’s Speaker of Parliament said the violation makes it “unreasonable” to continue negotiations with the U.S. ahead of upcoming talks surrounding a longer ceasefire. 

Vance is expected to lead upcoming peace talks in Islamabad later this week and worked with China’s officials to secure the initial phase of the ceasefire, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters on Wednesday.

data.title: Democratic senator: Hegseth 'blowing smoke' with claims about Iran war
data.description:
Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.) on Wednesday accused Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth of “blowing smoke” over his claims about the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran.

Van Hollen slammed Hegseth after the secretary praised the military operation against Iran “a historic and overwhelming victory” earlier in the day on Wednesday. He said at a Pentagon briefing that “Operation Epic Fury” was a “capital V military victory by any measure” and that Iran’s military was “decimated” and “combat ineffective for years to come.”

“Pete Hegseth is, once again, blowing smoke, trying to deceive the American people,” Van Hollen told CNN’s Kate Bolduan. “Look, there’s no doubt that we are worse off today than when Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Bibi Netanyahu started this war.”

He added that Iran has established that it can control the Strait of Hormuz, having closed it at the start of the conflict, reopened it after reaching a ceasefire deal with the U.S. on Tuesday and then closed it again Wednesday, citing Israeli strikes on Lebanon.

“We have a regime in Iran that is more radical, more extreme, more hardline than the one before, and Donald Trump, in his post yesterday, said that we’re going to be working off Iran’s 10-point plan going forward,” the Maryland Democrat continued.

Van Hollen added that the U.S. is “less safe today than when this war started, and we are worse off.”

“This has been a complete and total disaster, costing the American taxpayers billions of dollars per day and, of course, disrupting our economy,” he said.

Iran appeared to back off the ceasefire deal Wednesday afternoon. Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf, the speaker of Iran’s Parliament, said in a statement that the “‘workable bases on which to negotiate’ has openly and clearly violated, even before the negotiations began,” referring to Trump’s label that Iran’s 10-point peace plan was “workable.”

“In such situations, a bilateral ceasefire or negotiations is unreasonable,” Ghalibaf stated.

Iran also shut down the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday after briefly reopening it the day before. Ghalibaf cited continued Israeli attacks on Lebanon, which was a component of the peace deal.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said a cessation of hostilities in Lebanon was not part of what their countries agreed to from Iran’s plane.

Lebanese Minister of Social Affairs Haneen Sayed told The Associated Press that Israel’s wave of airstrikes amid the ceasefire deal set a “dangerous turning point.” Israel carried out a barrage of strikes on 100 sites in 10 minutes on Wednesday.

“These hits are now at the heart of Beirut. … Half of the sheltered (internally displaced people) are in Beirut in this area,” he told the AP.

data.title: Democrats to try limiting Trump Iran war powers during House pro forma session
data.description:
Democrats will attempt to pass a resolution to limit President Trump’s war powers in Iran by unanimous consent during a pro forma session of the House on Thursday.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) announced the plan in a letter to colleagues on Wednesday while criticizing Trump’s management of the conflict and the ceasefire deal announced Tuesday.

“A two-week ceasefire is woefully insufficient. Accordingly, we have demanded that the House come back into session immediately in order to vote on our resolution to permanently end the war in the Middle East,” Jeffries said.

“To the extent Republican leadership refuses to bring Congress back this week, House Democrats will demand passage of the War Powers Resolution introduced by [House Foreign Affairs Committee] Ranking Member Greg Meeks [D-N.Y.] via unanimous consent at the next pro forma session on Thursday, April 9 at 11:30 a.m. sharp,” Jeffries said.

The House is the middle of a two-week recess, with votes next scheduled on April 14.

Pro forma sessions of the House typically consist of a member gaveling in and out of session and last for mere minutes, conducted for reasons related to appointments and Constitutional requirements.

Republican leadership is almost certain to bat down the unanimous consent request.

Jeffries said that Rep. Glenn Ivey (D-Md.) will lead the effort seeking unanimous consent to pass the resolution on the House floor, and invited all members who are in Washington to join.

The House in March defeated a resolution that would force Trump to terminate military operations against Iran until the administration could get congressional approval. Two Republicans supported it then, while four Democrats opposed it.

Democrats have long planned to force another vote on a resolution limiting Trump’s war powers when the House is back in session, and believe that with the change in the political environment, they can get enough support to pass it. Several of the Democrats who initially opposed the measure are poised to support it now, and Republicans can afford no more than one defection assuming all members are present and voting.

Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.), meanwhile, announced Wednesday that the upper chamber would vote on an Iran war powers resolution when Congress returned next week.

Jeffries also announced that House Judiciary Committee ranking member Jamie Raskin (D-Md.) will hold a virtual briefing Friday “on Trump administration accountability and the 25th Amendment.” The briefing comes after a wave of House Democrats called for Trump to be removed via the 25th Amendment process over a Truth Social post saying that “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again” if Iran did not meet his deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. 

“Shockingly, Donald Trump threatened to escalate his war of choice in a profane Easter Sunday rant and to eradicate an entire civilization. House Republican leadership remains completely silent on the president’s unhinged behavior. Instead, they continue to enable and excuse his dangerous conduct. We will continue to unleash maximum pressure on Republicans to put patriotic duty over party loyalty and join Democrats in stopping the madness,” Jeffries said.

data.title: Trump: 'Fraudsters' circulating purported Iran talks documents
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday claimed “fraudsters” were circulating documents related to negotiations between the U.S. and Iran over the countries’ conflict.

“Numerous Agreements, Lists, and Letters are being sent out by people that have absolutely nothing to do with the U.S.A. / Iran Negotiation, in many cases, they are total Fraudsters, Charlatans, and WORSE,” Trump said Wednesday afternoon on Truth Social. 

“They will be rapidly exposed after our Federal Investigation is completed. There is only one group of meaningful ‘POINTS’ that are acceptable to the United States, and we will be discussing them behind closed doors during these Negotiations,” he added.

CNN recently defended its reporting about a statement from Iranian officials about a ceasefire agreement the U.S. and Iran reached hours before Trump was set to begin an attack on Iranian infrastructure.

The outlet quoted Iranian officials claiming a “great victory” in the negotiations and painting the agreement as one Trump went along with to open the Strait of Hormuz so oil shipments could resume.

In an earlier Truth Social post, Trump went after CNN for publishing the statement, which other news outlets also cited.

“The alleged Statement put out by CNN World News is a FRAUD, as CNN well knows. The false Statement was linked to a Fake News site (from Nigeria) and, of course, immediately picked up by CNN, and blared out as a ‘legitimate’ headline,” Trump said.

CNN said “the statement in question was obtained by CNN from Iranian officials and reported on multiple Iranian state media outlets.”

“We received the statement from specific official Iranian spokespeople who are known to us,” it added.

Trump, in his Wednesday afternoon Truth Social post, went after CNN again, accusing it of “headlining a ‘source’ that had no power or authority to write a Letter claiming great authority.”

The Hill has reached out to CNN for comment.

data.title: Fired University of Wisconsin system president 'blindsided' by ouster
data.description:
Former Universities of Wisconsin President Jay Rothman, who was fired on Tuesday, said he was shocked by the decision, but that he was unlikely to pursue a lawsuit.

“Absolutely I was blindsided,” Rothman told The Associated Press in his first interview since the ouster, saying he had still not been given a reason.

“I really don’t know,” he said. “I asked for reasons why. They were not able to articulate any.”

The system’s board of regents asked Rothman to retire or resign or face being fired late last week, according to the AP. He reportedly considered stepping down and sent two letters to the board requesting a reason but decided against it after not receiving one.

Rothman was dismissed from the role less than a week later, a move Republican state Senate President Patrick Testin labeled in a statement a “blatant partisan hatchet job.”

Rothman had faced some pushback from state Democrats after brokering a 2023 deal to freeze a certain number of diversity, equity and inclusion positions within the school system in exchange for state funding for pay raises, school projects and other initiatives.  

“When you come in to affect change and you try to move an organization forward, you have to make difficult decisions,” Rothman told the AP on Wednesday when asked whether the issue could have factored into his firing.

“And when you make difficult decisions, you can upset some people,” he said, later adding that he did not want to speculate on reasoning.

“I am disappointed with the board’s action, but I’m not angry,” he continued. “This is not about retribution. I’m concerned about the future of the Universities of Wisconsin.”

Regent President Amy Bogost told the AP in a statement before the firing that the decision was “about the future” of the Wisconsin university system, without delving into specifics about Rothman.

“The Universities of Wisconsin must be led with a clear vision that both protects and strengthens our flagship, supports our comprehensive universities and ensures we are meeting the evolving needs of our students, workforce and communities across all 72 counties,” Bogost said.

The personnel shake-up came two days ahead of a scheduled state Senate committee hearing for 10 regents appointed by Gov. Tony Evers (D), who are awaiting confirmation.

Testin called on his colleagues in a statement shared on the social platform X on Wednesday to reject “every single one of their confirmations.”

“As they say, actions have consequences,” he said.
